chunks.text: Hi, are we all in the right place? Ah, there we go. There's Tiano, and I hear David. Hi, Mr. Chairman. Just checking I was in the right place. Yes, you are in the right place. Fabulous. Now.
We're in public session now.
Just bear with us. Ah, here we go. Oh, I think they're on.

Morning, everyone. Good morning, Andy. I'm Alex. Oh, Alex. Sorry. Yeah. You're labeled as Andy. Right. We're doing a double act. Welcome to the Primary Production Select Committee. You have five minutes for your presentation. Take your submissions read.

So I'll give you a little bit of time to do your introductory comments, and then if you leave time for questions, that'd be really useful. So it's in your hands. Away you go. Okay. Yeah, good morning, everyone. My name's Alex. And my name is Andy, and we're bringing you an important message from the future.

But before we move forward in time, it's important to move back. I'd first of all like to paint a picture for you of our youth. We grew up in a land where the oceans were plentiful. We caught fish on every trip, some we kept and many we put back. These were sort of amazing days spent with family and friends. We caught fish, lobster, crab, often ending up on the beach in the evening beside a beachside fire.

But that was 35 years ago, and it wasn't in New Zealand. We grew up in Guernsey. Guernsey's a small island in the English Channel. Back then, it was a fishing mecca where people would come to go fishing and leave raving about the Channel Islands and the amazing catches they got there.

Now the strange thing is, back in our 20s, we started to notice a decline in the fish we were catching. Fewer fish and smaller fish. Meanwhile, out to sea, the commercial fleet were working hard. They were gillnetting and trawling, and it seemed fairly obvious to us that there was a decline. And talking to the old boys, they told us tales of even more astonishing abundance.

So clearly this decline wasn't a new thing. However, what we heard from the commercial fishing industry was calm assurance. "Don't worry, all is well," they told us. "We're fishing sustainably, trust us. Why do we want to overfish? It's our livelihood. We've run the numbers and we've engaged the scientists and the stocks are perfectly healthy and there are plenty more fish in the sea."

So you can see the startling similarity with what we heard 30 years ago and what we're hearing in New Zealand now. Once again, we're being told the seas are healthy, when to all of us it seems they're not. We've been presented with a Fisheries Amendment Bill that will prioritize exports, profit, and private interests, a bill that will certainly accelerate the take of ocean resources whilst diminishing the opportunity for any of us to rally against it. Now here's the interesting thing.

What if we could jump forward 30 years and see what the effects of massive overfishing can do to an ecosystem, a population, and an entire economy? Well, we can't look at New Zealand right now, but we can look at Guernsey and the rest of Europe. How have their oceans fared in the last 30 years since? Unfortunately, not very well at all.

I visited the island again last year and the seas are virtually barren. I dived the same areas that I dived in my youth and it's completely changed. I was gutted. Practically any fish of edible size has gone, both the high value and the low value species. Now is this what will happen in New Zealand? It's very hard to say.

But I think one thing we can be assured of is that this Fisheries Amendment Bill will increase the likelihood of that happening here. And it seems to me we've gone beyond the canary in the coal mine stage. We've already fished out the northeast coast scallops and the crayfish, the Hauraki mussels have gone. The Hapuka are only seen offshore in small numbers and many species are well below 50% of their original biomass. So do you see a pattern emerging here? Can you see the similarities with Guernsey and many other ocean nations around the world?

But it's important also to look beyond the sea life and look to the people, those who enjoy the ocean and the businesses that depend upon it. Back in Guernsey, the bays which once bustled with small boats are now quiet. Many of the tackle shops and marine stores, they went out of business. And our friends and family who all used to go fishing and boating together no longer bother. What's the point of going out to sea when there's no fish to catch?

And remember the social impact of this. All those fabulous days that we spent as kids boating, fishing, barbecuing our fresh catch and sharing it with our families. These are no longer a thing and a whole way of life has been completely extinguished. And there's one more point I'd like to make, that if this bill is to be decided on economics alone, then know this, the Guernsey fishing industry has collapsed despite their unfounded assurances that all would be okay.

And where do the people of Guernsey now go to buy their fish? Well, they go to the supermarket and yes, you've guessed it, they buy hoki from New Zealand. We realize we're not in Guernsey or Europe. We're on the other side of the world, but there are many, many parallels with other ocean nations who've suffered systemic declines in marine species. So you, the decision makers, have a chance here today to reverse this trend. So we implore you to vote for the people.

And vote for the ocean And vote for a better future than the one that we have already experienced. We believe that we need to vote, and you need to vote to kill the entire Fisheries Amendment Bill. Thank you. Thank you. Look, I'm afraid we've come to the five-minute mark already.

That's all right. But I really appreciate the effort that you've made to put a submission in, and thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Right. Moving on. The next submitter is Ben Dobbie. Come on up, Ben. Just- Where do you want me to stand? If you sit in the middle chair at the front here.

Oh, right. In front of the microphone. Yeah. Right. Right up here. Yep. Got you. Welcome- Hello... to the Select Committee. Thank you for your submission. Take your submission as read. Five minutes goes very quickly. If you'd like some questions, leave some time for those, and away you go.

Thank you very much. I'm a little underprepared member of the public who, um, this is my first experience being in Parliament, so thank you for your consideration. So I just wanted to talk to a few sections in the bill, particularly about restricting camera access onboard ships.

I believe that this accountability is essential given the public nature of the oceans and the public interest in the oceans. I think we need to have that accountability of having camera access onboard ships. I also think that the five-year system, the multi-year catch decision five-year system is too inflexible for what we require. We need to be able to respond to the scientific data and what that tells us about our ecosystems.

I recommend very much that this bill be scrapped. I've recently seen in examples of documentaries of how protecting even small amounts of our coastline can spill over into other areas and can benefit all the surrounding areas. Doing this would benefit everyone, hobby fishermen, environmentalists like myself, and even the commercial fishing industry.

Okay. We need to go beyond just scrapping this bill, but also to adhere to the UN target of 30% protected oceans by 2030, which I might add the government has signed on to. Thank you. Very good. All right. Could you delve a bit more into the camera access question and- Yes... what you think looks good, and what you think what you're not happy with, with what has been proposed?

So what I'm not happy with is the proposed blocking of public access to camera footage, and that the potential for fines for misuse of camera footage. I think it needs to be public information given that what is being fished is relevant to the public. Everything in our oceans is a shared resource, and I think we have the right to know what's being taken out of our oceans.

Do you think, just playing devil's advocate here, that recreational fishers should have a camera on their boats as well? I can't speak to that too much. I personally don't fish. I'm vegan. I would rather that nobody took any fish out of the ocean, but I realize that that's unrealistic.

I think, though, that the volumes that are being fished by the commercial fishing industry mean that they need that requirement of public accountability because the volumes that they're taking are far larger than the hobby fishing industry.

Mr. Chair, can I ask- We have a question from Rachel Boyack. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thank you for your submission. I'm just Zooming in here from Nelson. Question I've got is, I guess, around you've talked about transparency, but also, what about the privacy of those who are working on the ships? And actually, sometimes the vessels are quite small and for some people working in the sector, it's actually where they're living and having their break time as well. So I'm just interested how you balance that transparency with protection for people who are just doing their

jobs. I think it would depend where you could put the cameras on the ships so that they're not invading people's personal space, but still providing information on what is being caught. I personally feel that it is more important, if you are going on to a fishing ship to catch fish that is essentially a public resource, then I think it's incumbent upon you to essentially lose a bit of

privacy in that. Okay. I guess if you looked at other industries, though, where, say let's look at, um, uh-People who work in milking sheds. There's public interest there, but there aren't cameras that are then able to be released to the public of people doing their jobs.

So, would you see this as something that needs to be extended into other industries? Yes, possibly. Yep. Yeah. Look, thanks. We've come to time, so it goes very quickly. Thank you very much for your time, Ian. Awesome. And have a great day.
Thank you very much. Right. Moving on. We now have Gilbert Peterson.

Welcome, Gilbert. Kia ora. The time goes fast, as you noticed. Yep. So, I suggest you keep your introductory remarks short if you would like questions. So it's- Sure... in your hands. So I want to talk about the context, the broader issues, really using a couple of examples to sort of amplify what I said in my submission.

For many years, I worked at the Employers and Manufacturers Association in Auckland, and more recently I edited and published Dive Pacific Magazine. During my time at EMA, one thing I learned was that innovation is the mother and father of productivity on which our economic growth depends, and I fail to see much innovation in this bill. And in fact, to me, it looks very 19th century.

Several Nobel laureates have explored and demonstrated the economics of how innovation and productivity work. During my time at Dive Pacific Magazine, I learned a lot, too. I wanted to share two examples because they're about whales and bottom trawling.

They're the context for what we're talking about. In Dive Pacific, we published articles by such as Jean-Michel Cousteau, and one of these on the trophic cascades of whales, I think illustrates just how 19th century the thinking behind this bill is.

Back then, in the 19th century, we all know we hunted and killed lots of whales, so much so their numbers have yet to recover from 150 years ago. And in fact, back then, by killing whales, we were actually harming ourselves and our ability to sustain ourselves through the food that the whales are actually releasing back into the food chain. The baleen whales, the really big ones, in particular, they go down deep and eat krill, phytoplankton, and they bring it up and they come to the surface and they distribute vast amounts of fecal matter.

And that fecal matter fertilizes the phytoplankton at the surface. The phytoplankton can't live without light. And that phytoplankton provides food for the krill, and so the cycle goes on. And all the understanding of this trophic cascade, it hasn't really been understood or explored at all much until the last few decades.

So, by killing the whales, we were actually harming our own ability to survive, and I think there's a lesson in that. Who would've thought? I would hope that a more thoughtful amendment to the Fisheries Act in future will take into account marine science such as this, rather than apparently disregard it.

But we're only beginning to understand some of this stuff. The second example is bottom trawling. In 2021, Nature magazine, which is a highly reputable magazine, published an article that reported bottom trawling scrapes 1.9 million square miles of seabed each year. New Zealand makes its contribution to this, of course, and it releases vast amounts of carbon that would otherwise stay in the seabed indefinitely.

In that report, Nature calculated the amount of carbon so released exceeds that of Germany, or about the same amount as international world travel globally. Who would've thought that? So, whales and bottom trawling are just examples. Undersized fish. Anyway, here today we're going to discuss the merits of catching and selling undersized fish. How did that happen? I find that incredibly embarrassing.

And where are the incentives listed anywhere for there to be development of fishing capture methods which allow small fish to escape and become bigger fish worth catching and eating? Cameras on fishing boats. I don't doubt that responsible fisher people are okay with those cameras and with the images from them, for them to become available to the public.

The cameras protect the fisher people as much as they do the fish. And the taxpayer currently is funding most of those cameras. So this bill is telling us we, the taxpayer, we're being required to pay for these cameras and these images, but we're not allowed to see them. So we're being told we can pay, but we can't actually use or see.

It's like taxation without representation. It's the very antithesis of what our democracy is based on I find in the bill there are many other flaws, and I would urge the select committee to recommend that it go no further. Thanks, Gilbert. Our time has come to an end.
I appreciate you making the effort to come and submit. Very well. Thank you. Thank you. Right. Next, we have Mae Dobbie.

Morning, Mae. How are you? Welcome to the select committee. Congratulations on taking part in the democratic process. You've got five minutes, and as you've seen, it takes just a blink of the eye for the five minutes to go past, so away you go.

Hi. My name is Mae. I'm nine, and I'm from Whakatane. I think it would be dumb if this bill comes in. Our oceans are already in a terrible state. We should be protecting them, not making it even easier for fishing boats to ruin them. We're on track to a dead ocean. Do we want to make it worse? What I don't like about this bill is that the Minister of Fisheries can make decisions about how much the fishing boats are allowed to catch, and the environment is not really the main thing that is considered when making those decisions.

Penalties for bad practice have also been lowered. I also don't like that when there are cameras on the boat, the footage won't really be able to be used, and those who share it can be fined heaps. We need camera footage to be accessible, because if the fishing companies know the footage won't be available, they'll ignore it and do what they like.

A while ago, the Ministry for Primary Industries went through some footage, and found that there had been much more dolphins, whales, and other animals killed by fishing boats than fishing companies said. We know these commercial fishing boats lie. Why would we make it even easier for them to do so? Also, if there are plenty of cameras on the boats with easily accessed footage, it will make it safer for people on the fishing boats who get abused.

This is a very big problem on fishing boats. And New Zealand is the only country in the world that allows bottom trawling in the South Pacific. Bottom trawling is like bulldozing the ocean floor. It destroys everything in its path, including coral. Coral takes a really, really long time to grow and will take a long time to grow back, all for the sake of money.

Allowing fishing companies more control over how they fish will likely lead to more bottom trawling. In New Zealand, we claim to be clean and green. This is a lie. Is the government trying to destroy the ocean? We need to be prioritizing the environment and the world generations after you're living, not prioritizing a few rich guys' pockets.

We need to object this bill entirely. We also need to ban bottom trawling. Shouldn't we be ashamed to be the last country bottom trawling in the South Pacific? As a vegan, I don't think we should have any commercial fishing in any part of our oceans. But I know that won't happen, so I suggest that we do what the UN and heaps of charities and scientists say we should do, and protect at least 30% of it. In NZ, we protect about 1% at the moment.

We need to defend our ocean, and if we work together and do our bit, we can save the ocean. Thank you for listening. Thank you very much. Now, does any of the committee members have any questions? Teanaru. Kia ora, Mae. Thank you very much for a very good submission. It was very clear, and we heard your concerns. I was wondering, if you were to look forward into the future, say, 10 years into the future, what would you like to see in terms of the health of our ocean?

Well, I would like to see an ocean with lots of healthy coral and fish, and a good ecosystem. So, yeah. Rachel. Hi, Mae. I just wanted to say thank you very much for your awesome submission. And I've got a question for you. Have you had the opportunity to go snorkeling and go looking at places where there are lots of fish in the ocean, so you can enjoy that, or is that something you still got to get an opportunity to do?

That's something that I've still got an opportunity to do, but I have once gone and sort of saw them and seaweed underneath the water. But yeah. That sounds really cool. I hope you get that opportunity one day to go snorkeling and to look at some of the fish in marine reserves and stuff, because it's really awesome. I can imagine.

We should try and make that happen. All right. Yeah. Yeah. Are you offering, Rachel? Oh, I think we should talk to the local MPs for you, and we can have a chat, because this year I got to go and see some snapper at the Leigh Marine Reserve, and it was awesome. So-... I think that's something they should help you do. I'll have a chat to them.
Very good. All right. There doesn't seem to be any more questions, so thank you very much for your submission, Mae. Thank you for listening. Very good. Right. Moving on. We now have Rebecca Bruno.

Mōrena. Good morning, Rebecca. Welcome to the Primary Production select committee. Thank you. You've seen how it works. Seen how it works. Tough act to follow. So, I'll leave it with you. All right. Tena koutou. Mōrena. Thank you for the opportunity to speak today. Recently, I watched my lovely five-year-old daughter catch her first fish. It was a small one, although you wouldn't know it from the retelling of our stories. Each time the story is told, it grows a little bigger.

The story's a little more magical.Now that moment mattered and I want that same moment, that same connection to our oceans to be there for my grandchildren and for theirs. We are not users and takers of these resources. We are kaitiaki. We need to protect them. I want to acknowledge the importance of this bill, and New Zealand's export economy does rely heavily on our fishing industry, and I'm actually fully supportive of a thriving, successful sector, but the success must be sustainable. I am concerned that the bill reduces transparency

and oversight and shifts the focus towards short-term gains from commercial fishing. I support a strong and thriving industry, but not at the cost of future fish stocks and not at the cost of my children's opportunity to fish. Sustainability is not an opposition to economic success, it's what enables it. If we take too much, too fast or without the right safeguards, we don't just risk the environment, we risk the long-term viability of the industry itself, and we risk something that's harder to measure, the connection between our people and the ocean and between people and the environment.

New Zealand has an opportunity here. Why can't we lead with an approach that is innovative, future-focused and genuinely sustainable? An approach that supports the fishing industry to adapt, evolve and lead rather than prioritize short-term financial gains. Sustainability should not be seen as a constraint. It should be seen as a driver of innovation.

So all I wish to leave you with is this: we can fish for today or we can fish for tomorrow. We can take what we can or we can take what we should. If we get this right, we don't have to choose between the economy and the environment. We can protect both. I urge the committee to ensure that this bill, if it continues, supports a fishing industry that is not just successful but is sustainable for our generations to come.

Thank you, Rebecca. Does the committee have any questions? I see Rachel seems to have dropped off. No, I'm here. I've just got my camera off briefly. I am here. Oh, okay. Sorry. Teanau, did you have a question? No, that was a very clear submission which I agree with.

Okay. Well, I do. Just what particular part of the bill do you think is the most egregious in your opinion? I think that's a very good question. I think the changing around catch limits. I think it's obvious to let fish grow. And I know that there's been a lot of focus on the camera pieces, and we could argue that, and I do think that that's actually perhaps the noise around that cloud some of the other details.

So I would say for me it's sizes and catch limits. We need to make sure that we're following the science. Right. And whatever method is, whether it's cameras or something else, we need to make sure that we follow those. So. So you may have seen last week we had the minister in front of the committee.

And his stated intention was that the size currently, he contends that a number of undersized fish will perish even if they're released back into the ocean. So the intention is to include that as part of their catch against their quota. Mm.

So they're not releasing the undersized fish- Mm-hmm... that die anyway. Sure. And then top up with legal fish. Yes. I guess what question is, would that be acceptable, do you think? I think the question is why are we catching the small fish in the first place?

Why can't we have a method where we only take-- Why are we bulk catching fish? I think that's been raised here around we're the only nation still trawling in the South Pacific. So if we are catching smaller fish that are gonna die regardless of what we do, why are we doing practices that are allowing that anyway? So I think it's a bigger upstream problem.

I think what you say is sensible. If we are going to trawl and we are gonna take everything, then everything you take is against your catch. But I think we should look at the wider problem is why are we taking those fish in the first place. Very good. Thank you very much, Rebecca. I appreciate you taking the time. Moving on now. Our next submitters are all by Zoom.
So we've got Kiri Moran.
Good morning, Kiri.

Oops. That might be helpful, mightn't it, if I have my camera on. Ah. Sorry. Apologies. I thought I did already. Yeah. Anyway, I'll start from scratch. Yes, please. Tena koutou katoa. Thank you for the opportunity to speak today. I should clarify from the outset that my interest in this bill is driven solely by its environmental and public accountability implications. I'm shocked by the legislation with such serious implications for our marine ecosystems is even being considered.

It is no surprise then that I do not support the Fisheries Amendment Bill. Further, I express my strong support for the positions put forward by Legacy and Forest and Bird, whose submissions highlight fundamental flaws in the Fisheries Amendment Bill, which I won't be able to cover everything todayI want to make four key points today.

First, this bill weakens environmental protections at a time when we need stronger safeguards. New Zealand's marine ecosystems are already under enormous pressure from climate change, habitat loss, and decades of overfishing. Instead of strengthening regulatory framework, this bill loosens it. It creates pathways for increased catch, reduced scrutiny, and more discretionary decision-making, all at the expense of ecological resilience.

From an environmental perspective, this is moving in the wrong direction. Second, it reduces transparency. One of the most concerning aspects is the shift away from independent verification and towards more closed-door processes. Restricting access to onboard camera reporting requirements undermine the transparency that New Zealanders have repeatedly said they want in fisheries management.

If the system is functioning well, transparency should be welcomed, not avoided. Three, it concentrates power and reduces democratic oversight. The bill grants the minister greater authority while reducing the checks and balances that ensure decisions are grounded in environmental science and public interest.

This is not a minor procedural adjustment. It's a structural change that risks sidelining the voices of communities, scientists, and environmental advocates. New Zealanders have consistently shown that they want sustainable fisheries and strong environmental protections. This bill moves us further away from this expectation.

Four, it privileges short-term commercial interests over long-term public good and environmental sustainability. Our oceans are a shared resource. When legislation prioritizes the interests of a small number of quota holders over the health of marine ecosystems and the rights of future generations, such as the gorgeous Mae, something is fundamentally out of balance.

A sustainable fishery is one where the environment comes first, because without a healthy ecosystem, there is no fishery at all. In closing, I want to leave you with this thought. Aotearoa's marine environment is one of our greatest taonga. You have the chance today to protect something that cannot speak for itself, but that future generations will depend on.

I hope you choose the path that honors that responsibility. Nga mihi nui. Thank you. Does the committee have any questions? Teanau. Thank you very much for your very comprehensive submission. I was interested to know a bit more about your perspectives on your point number two around transparency and verification of quotas and stuff.

In your mind, what does that tell New Zealand when that power is strengthened? In my mind, it weakens the democratic process for a start. To me, it shows that something underhanded is going on, that not everything is, obviously, as I said in my statement, that not everything is completely transparent. So things like bycatch, for instance, all these beautiful marine mammals.

And it's not being reported, so potentially that could mean that we don't know the reason why the numbers of these beautiful marine mammals are declining. If we can actually see that actually the-- Sorry, I'm a little bit nervous. It's the first time I've done this, so.

Kerri, I just have to interrupt you there. We've exceeded time. Can I just ask a quick sup though, just on that? No. Thank you. We've run out of time. I'm sorry, Rachel. We'll just have to move on. Okay. No, I just wanted to clarify, the MPI do actually release the data on what's caught. They actually release that in paper form. Great. So, thank you for your submission.

Thank you. And you shouldn't need to be nervous. Teanau doesn't bite. You're all right. Thank you. No worries. Moving on. We now have Cam McLeod on Zoom as well. Teanau, your sound quality's really bad. Yeah. It is very hard to hear you, and I would think that when you're asking a question, those that have been asked will find it difficult to hear you as well.
Maybe I'll try again. Yeah, sorry. It's apart of my laptop. Oh, look. Technical stuff, mate. So is Cam online? Just trying to prompt him to accept the motion.
Are they came.
A few technical issues here, team.

Good morning, all. Good morning I've just been rejoined as a panelist. Yes. Good morning and welcome to the Select Committee. Thank you. You have five minutes, and the time is yours to use how you wish. But if you want questions, I suggest leave a good amount of time at the end. Away you go.

Thank you, Miles. Yeah. So as my submission says, I'll open by simply saying, I do not support the Fisheries Amendment Bill 2026 because it quietly shifts control of a public resource away from the public. The submitters, or the majority of the submitters I would say, representing tens of thousands of New Zealanders, reject the bill entirely.

Not because the fisheries don't need improvement, but because this bill doesn't fix the real problem. The current law is already strong. The failure has been in how it's applied. And instead of improving implementation, this bill rewrites the rules, so past failures become acceptable. Now here's the important part.

You can't understand this bill by looking at one clause at a time. And in fact, Miles, you spoke to some earlier submitters and asked them, "What aspect of the bill do you not like?" It's the whole thing, because the changes seem technical, but they form a pattern. Environmental protections are narrowed, public input is reduced, accountability is weakened, and legal challenge becomes harder. Taken together, this is what's known as regulatory capture, where the industry being regulated gains influence over the rules themselves.

Okay? Take a look at what gets overlooked. The bill limits how environmental factors are considered when setting catch limits. It focuses on large management areas, which can hide local depletion. So while numbers may look stable on paper, entire local ecosystems can be quietly degrading.

Even more concerning, the less we know about a fish stock, the weaker the legal protection becomes. That reverses a basic principle of good management. Uncertainty should lead to caution, not leniency. The bill also shifts how decisions are made. It allows reliance on industry catch data as a primary signal.

It introduces preset rules run by officials instead of ministers, and it leans on voluntary industry measures instead of enforceable regulation. On the surface, that sounds efficient, but when you look closer, it moves decision-making away from independent oversight towards systems influenced by the very groups that benefit from higher catch limits.

Then there are the incentives, the quiet drivers of behavior. The bill allows more discarding of fish at sea. It enables unused quota to be carried forward in large amounts, and it reduces certain penalties. None of these sound dramatic on their own, but together they encourage overfishing, waste, and delayed response to declining stocks.

At the same time, transparency is reduced. Camera footage from fishing vessels can be withheld. Public consultation is limited, and the timeframe to legally challenge decisions is cut to just 20 days. So even if something goes wrong, it becomes much harder to see it, question it, and stop it.

So step back and a deeper pattern emerges. When you look at the whole picture, decisions are made earlier, public input comes later or not at all, and legal challenge is constrained. This locks in commercial interests first and limits the abilities of others to respond.

So this isn't just a technical fisheries bill. It's a shift in who holds power over a shared natural resource. The real question isn't whether this improves efficiency. The real question is: who are our fisheries being managed for? Because when you look closely, the biggest change here isn't in the wording of the law, it's in who the system ultimately serves.

It moves decision-making away from independent oversight towards systems influenced by the very groups that benefit from higher catch limits. This bill should be rejected outright. Good government is about regulating excessive imbalance of power in society. I think it is entirely legitimate that owners of fishing quota are regulated to ensure the methods they use only catch the fish they are entitled to in the amounts they are entitled to without killing many other species and damaging the marine habitats.

Does owning fish quota allow you to wreck the place and kill indiscriminately? This bill is designed to allow and encourage commercial interests to do just that. And to come to Rachel's question earlier on about the milking shed. If the taxpayer or the cows in the milking shed were just out there to help yourself to, feel free.

Yeah, then maybe you should have cameras in milking sheds. But the cows are there because the farmer put the cows there. So if the fishing industry put the fish in the water, then maybe they'd have the right to talk about cameras. That's the end of my submission. Thank you, all. Thank you. We've come to time. Oh. So, it goes quick.
It does. But thanks for making the effort today. And we'll move on. Thank you, sir. Thank you. Right. Next submission is from Andrew Church.

Oh, Anthony Phipps. Anthony Phipps. So Andrew's not submitting. Yeah. I think that's his next submission. Oh, yeah. Anthony Phipps is the next submitter. There we are, Tony. Thanks for taking the time today. No. You have five minutes. Fire away.

Right. Kia ora koutou, and thank you for this opportunity to make a submission. And I'm glad we live in a country where people can do this and participate in the process. I do not support the bill. In fact, I strongly oppose it because I think at a high level it's going in the wrong direction, and that we really should be looking at strengthening, not weakening and undermining environmental protections and protection of the fisheries for

all New Zealanders and generations of New Zealanders to come. So I think at a high level, the purpose of the bill should be going in the other direction. And as you've heard from many submitters, the values that New Zealanders hold around fisheries are much, much wider and different to just the commercial values.
